title: Simple pine wall shelf
subtitle: A single-shelf wall bracket in softwood with through-tenon bracket joints
excerpt: A single pine wall shelf with two bracket supports is one of the clearest introductions to hand-tool woodwork: a tenon saw, a chisel, and a brace and bit cover all the joinery needed. The bracket-to-shelf joint is a through tenon driven from below, which is visible on the shelf top and functions as a design feature as much as a structural element. This beginner project covers marking, sawing, chiselling the mortise, fitting the tenon, and applying a boiled linseed oil finish.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for bracket shelf construction. Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898) for the through-tenon joint in pine furniture.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber dried to a stable moisture content, dimensionally stable and ready for joinery. A seasoned shelf will not warp after the joints are cut.

A joint in which a rectangular projection (tenon) on one piece fits into a matching slot (mortise) in the other. The through tenon in this project passes completely through the shelf and is visible on the top face.
A tool with an adjustable fence and a scribing pin used to mark a line parallel to a face or edge at a consistent distance.

content.text: A wall shelf in
seasoned
pine teaches the core hand-tool sequence without requiring expensive hardwood: the
marking gauge
lays out the joint lines, the tenon saw cuts to those lines, and the chisel removes the mortise waste. The
mortise and tenon
joint needs no glue in pine at this scale; a tight fit held by gravity and the shelf's own weight is enough for a wall-hung display shelf.
What you need: wood

Shelf board: 60 cm long, 15 cm deep, 18 mm thick, from planed kiln-dried pine or PAR (planed all round) softwood. Two bracket boards: each 20 cm tall, 12 cm deep at the base, 18 mm thick, with the front face cut to a curve or angle. All dimensions from the timber merchant in finished size; no re-sawing required.
What you need: tools

A try square to mark lines 90 degrees to the face. A marking gauge for parallel lines. A tenon saw for the tenon shoulders and cheeks. A chisel and mallet for the mortise. A brace and bit to bore the waste from the mortise (optional but faster). Sandpaper at 120 and 180 grit. Boiled linseed oil.
Safety
Secure the workpiece in a vice or against a bench hook before sawing. Chisel cuts directed away from the body with the workpiece clamped on the bench.
The work
Step 1: Mark the mortise positions on the shelf

Mark the bracket positions on the underside of the shelf: 5 cm in from each end. At each position, mark a mortise: 35 mm long by 18 mm wide (to match the bracket thickness), centred on the shelf width. Use the try square to carry the lines across the shelf, and the marking gauge to mark the mortise width parallel to the shelf edges.
Step 2: Cut the mortises

Bore a series of holes inside the mortise outline with the brace and bit to remove the bulk of the waste. Pare the walls of the mortise to the marked lines with the chisel, working from both faces toward the centre to avoid splitting out the opposite face. The mortise should be exactly square to the shelf face; check with the try square.
Step 3: Cut the tenons on the bracket tops

Mark the tenon on the top of each bracket: 35 mm long (to pass through the shelf), 18 mm wide, centred on the bracket thickness. Saw the shoulder cuts (across the grain) with the tenon saw, then the cheek cuts (along the grain). The tenon should fit the mortise with hand pressure; a mallet-tight fit will do for assembly, but an over-tight fit risks splitting the shelf.
Step 4: Fit and finish

Drive each bracket tenon through its mortise from below until the bracket shoulder seats against the shelf underside. Sand all visible surfaces with 120 then 180 grit. Apply boiled linseed oil to all faces; two thin coats, 30 minutes apart. Fix the shelf to the wall through the bracket backs with appropriate wall fixings.
Variations
Oak brackets and shelf give a shelf that will last 50 or more years indoors; the through tenon is particularly attractive in figured oak.
A peg driven through the tenon from the top of the shelf locks the bracket permanently. Bore a 6 mm hole through the tenon end, drive a tapered hazel peg through it, and trim flush.
Care
Re-oil once a year if in a damp location such as a kitchen or bathroom. A well-fitted through tenon in pine needs no further attention.

items.symptom: The tenon is loose in the mortise
items.cause: The tenon cheeks were pared too thin when fitting.
items.fix: Glue a thin shim of matching wood to one tenon cheek, let it set, and re-fit. For future joints, test-fit frequently during paring rather than removing too much at once.

items.symptom: The mortise walls split when chiselling
items.cause: Chiselling in from one face only, so the chisel broke out the far face.
items.fix: Always work from both faces toward the centre for through mortises. On softwood especially, the near face will break out if the chisel is driven all the way through from one side.
